title: The Prefix Key
subtitle: Every tmux command starts with Ctrl-B (or whatever you remap it to)
summary: tmux commands are two-step: press the prefix key, release, then press the command key. Default prefix is Ctrl-B. Most users remap it because Ctrl-B clashes with Vim's page-up.

blocks.text: tmux's keymap is two-step on purpose. You press the prefix key — by default {key:Ctrl-B} — release it, and then press the actual command key. {key:Ctrl-B}{key:c} creates a window; {key:Ctrl-B}{key:%} splits a pane. The prefix exists because tmux runs *underneath* whatever program is in your active pane. Without a clear separator, tmux would have to fight every program for keystrokes. With a prefix, programs see all the normal keys; tmux sees only what comes after the prefix.

Press {key:Ctrl-B}{key:?} for the built-in cheat sheet. It lists every default binding in a scrollable buffer. Press {key:q} to dismiss it. This is the canonical "how do I do X in tmux" lookup — always a keystroke away.
Once you can get to {key:Ctrl-B}{key:?}, you can rediscover anything you forget about tmux. That single binding is worth memorizing first.

{key:Ctrl-B} clashes with Vim. Vim uses {key:Ctrl-B} for *page-back*. When Vim runs inside tmux, tmux intercepts the keystroke before Vim sees it, and Vim's page-up stops working. Three workarounds, in order of popularity:

rows: Cleanest. {key:Ctrl-Space} doesn't conflict with Vim or anything else common.
Familiar to screen users, but {key:Ctrl-A} shadows Vim's *increment number*.
No config change, but every page-up is two keystrokes instead of one.
Different Vim binding for the same operation
Acceptable if you only need page-up occasionally.

blocks.text: The remap-to-{key:Ctrl-Space} approach is the modern consensus. Almost every published `.tmux.conf` you'll find online does it. If you only learn one customization, learn this one.

blocks.title: Why Ctrl-B Was Picked Originally
blocks.text:
tmux inherited the convention of having a prefix from GNU screen, which used {key:Ctrl-A}. The prefix exists because tmux runs underneath whatever program is in the active pane; without a clear separator, every keystroke would have to be disambiguated. {key:Ctrl-B} was chosen because it looked unused.

It was, of course, not unused. Vim had been using it for page-back since the early 1990s, and screen users were already irritated by the {key:Ctrl-A} clash. The lesson is that there are no unused {key:Ctrl-letter} chords on a Unix keyboard — every one is contested by at least one popular program. The remap-to-{key:Ctrl-Space} convention took years to settle, and is now ubiquitous because {key:Ctrl-Space} *is* genuinely uncontested in most workflows.

blocks.title: Nicholas Marriott, screen, and the search for an unused chord
blocks.text:
tmux was written in 2007 by Nicholas Marriott, an OpenBSD developer who wanted *screen* — the venerable 1987 multiplexer — with a saner codebase and a BSD license. He copied screen's basic architecture (a server process, a client, a prefix key) and rewrote it cleanly. Almost everything you do in tmux still maps to a `screen` equivalent if you squint.

The prefix-key choice was the one place he deliberately broke from screen. screen used {key:Ctrl-A}, which fights Emacs's beginning-of-line. Nicholas picked the next letter — {key:Ctrl-B} — because it *looked* free. He hadn't realised Vim had been using {key:Ctrl-B} for page-up since 1991. Twenty years of Vim users discovered this the hard way: install tmux, run Vim, press {key:Ctrl-B} expecting page-up, watch tmux eat the keystroke. The fix has been the same .tmux.conf line in every tutorial since: `set -g prefix C-Space`. There is no shortcut left on a Unix keyboard that doesn't already belong to someone.
